raw_case:
The path of least resistance: The unusual extradigestive journey of a nasogastric tube following oropharyngeal perforation
Nasogastric tube placement is frequently performed in various medical settings. While generally deemed safe in patients without risk factors, complications may occur due to malposition, justifying the need of systematic confirmation of position with chest radiographs. We present the case of a critically ill male adult patient for whom the tube position was initially deemed very unusual on postinsertion radiographs, prompting further workup which ultimately confirmed an oropharyngeal perforation with a left parapharyngeal, left visceral, retrotracheal, and right retrodiaphragmatic course, and resulting in a recurrent pneumothorax and empyema treated by surgical decortication.

Introduction

Nasogastric tube (NGT) placement is frequently performed in various medical settings. Indications include medicine administration and/or short-term enteral nutrition in patients who cannot safely ingest food (such as in neurologically impaired patients), and gastric emptying (such as in patients with bowel obstruction) [1]. It is generally blindly placed by nurses and medical interns, and confirmation of good placement is routinely obtained using chest and abdominal radiographs [2]. We present the case of a critically ill male adult patient for whom the NGT position was deemed atypical on confirmation radiographs, prompting further workup which confirmed an oropharyngeal perforation with left parapharyngeal, left visceral, retrotracheal and right retrodiaphragmatic course, and which was complicated by a persistent pneumothorax and empyema despite chest tube installation, ultimately requiring decortication.

Case report

A 51-year-old man was initially transferred to the Intensive Care Unit (ICU) of our tertiary care institution for complications following abdominal hernia repair surgery including eventration, mesenteric laceration and small bowel necrosis. Intestinal resection and cure of eventration have been performed in a multistep approach. The patient was extubated on postoperative day 2, and an NGT was replaced by the medical team. Following that, the patient experienced a severe episode of desaturation and pain, and a chest X-ray revealed a right moderate pneumothorax that was subsequently addressed by placing a chest tube (Fig. 1A-B). The distal end of the NGT projected towards the right hypochondriac region, suggesting misplacement despite technical bedside imaging limitations. A Neck, Chest and Abdomen CT with contrast was then ordered to confirm position. The neck CT showed an aberrant course of the tube inserted via the right nares (Fig. 2A), perforating the left oropharynx likely at the level of the pharyngeal recess, and extending downward through the left parapharyngeal space (Fig. 2B). The tube then continued in the left visceral space of the neck, along the left lateral side of the hyoid bone, thyroid cartilage, and left thyroid lobe (Fig. 2C), before taking a posteromedial retropharyngeal and retrotracheal trajectory (Fig. 2D). The chest CT showed the incorrect position of the NGT throughout the right paraesophageal mediastinal soft tissue (Fig. 2D), with its distal end located in the right retrodiaphragmatic region (Fig. 3A-C). The full cervical and thoracic courses of the NGT are better visualized in Fig. 2E and Fig. 3D-E respectively. Mild emphysema and pneumomediastinum were noted along the tube's path, as well as a right moderate pleural effusion (Fig. 1B, Fig. 2D). In light of these findings, the medical team was emergently notified, and an ENT consultation was recommended. The patient was reintubated primarily for pain management. Unfortunately, the NGT was removed by the medical team before ENT evaluation, and no site of perforation was formally identified on subsequent flexible nasopharyngoscopy. Follow-up CT scans showed persistent right pneumothorax despite chest tube placement, as well as a right moderate expansive pleural collection suggesting empyema. Thoracic surgery consultation was sought, and a right pleural decortication procedure was performed. Cultures obtained perioperatively confirmed the presence of Curtibacterium acnes. The patient was then put under antibiotics and fared well following surgical treatment of his primary abdominal condition.

Discussion

NGT misplacement only occurs in 1%-3% of patients [3,4], yet remains commonly encountered given the numerous occurrences of daily placements in virtually all medical facilities. The most common type of misplacement involves the NGT being either too proximal (e.g., in the esophagus) or too distal (e.g., after the gastric antrum) but remaining confined in the gastrointestinal tract, with minimal adverse effects and easy management by the medical team by pushing or pulling the tube by a few centimeters. Misplacement related to tracheobronchial intubation is the second most common type and is associated with the highest number of serious adverse events [5]. It holds a high risk of aspiration and pneumonia when enteral feeding and medication are administered [6]. Pneumothorax, pleural effusion and empyema were also described [7]. This type can usually be easily diagnosed on postinsertion radiograph, further highlighting the importance of obtaining one in a timely manner and correctly interpreting it. The third type of misplacement, less frequent, involves a perforation at any level of the aerodigestive tract, from the nares to the stomach, either traumatically or secondary to altered postsurgical anatomy, which might lead to atypical (and sometimes challenging) positions on confirmation radiograph, as in our case. Risks factors include facial and skull base fractures (with intracranial malposition having been reported a few times [8,9]), history of radiotherapy or recent head and neck surgery, presence of endotracheal tube, and esophageal diseases (e.g., obstructive neoplasm, inflammatory esophagitis, etc.) [10]. Critically ill patients are particularly at high risk of any of these complications, given their altered mental status and often pre-existing endotracheal tube [11].In our case, the NGT traumatically perforated the left pharyngeal recess mucosa and undertook a path through the left parapharyngeal and left visceral spaces of the neck. Oddly enough, it then crossed midline through the retropharyngeal space and kept a right paraesophageal position throughout its thoracic descent. We can speculate that it might have followed the “path of least resistance” through the nearby, unrestrained adipose tissue, after being limited anterolaterally by the sternocleidomastoid and infra-hyoid muscles, and posteriorly by the deep cervical aponeurosis of the visceral space of the neck. Considering the downward retropharyngeal and retrotracheal course of the NGT up to the diaphragmatic region, we also speculate that it might have coursed in the low-resistance adipose tissue of the only anatomical neck space that has thoracic extension below T3-T4, the ``Danger space'', after having necessarily perforated the buccopharyngeal and alar fascias (perhaps of lesser resistance than the anterior aforementioned structures) during the NGT placement attempt. Ferrer-Inaebnit et al. [12] reported a similar case of NGT misplacement following oropharyngeal perforation, however with an intraperitoneal extraluminal abdominal course and without significant thoracic complications. Finally, despite the absence of endoscopic evidence of oropharyngeal perforation, it could certainly not be ruled out given the explicit suggestive imaging findings. Moreover, nasopharyngoscopy is an operator-dependent technique that can be of limited sensitivity in intubated patients, mainly because of the specific positioning of the tongue, the altered mental status of sedated patients, and the often-found abundant secretions [13]. Given these limitations, only the anterior pharyngeal wall could be adequately assessed by direct visualization.

Conclusion

NGT misplacement can lead to significant adverse effects, as demonstrated in our case. Confirmation of NGT adequate position is essential to allow prompt use of the tube and to avoid or allow timely management of complications. Special attention should be given to critically ill patients.

Author contributions

Conception and design: AB, RNG, ANW, MK, HL, MJD, PB, PPB.Data collection: AB, RNG, ANW, MK.Manuscript drafting: AB, RNG, ANW, MK, HL.Manuscript critical revision: AB, RNG, ANW, MK, HL, MJD, PB, PPB.Approval of the final version of manuscript: AB, RNG, ANW, MK, HL, MJD, PB, PPB.Agreement to be accountable for all aspects: AB, RNG, ANW, MK, HL, MJD, PB, PPB.

Patient consent

Written informed consent for publication was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 51-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint**: Complications following abdominal hernia repair surgery.
- **History of Present Illness**: The patient was admitted to the ICU due to postoperative complications from abdominal hernia repair, including eventration, mesenteric laceration, and small bowel necrosis. After intestinal resection and subsequent procedures, the patient was extubated on postoperative day 2 but experienced a severe desaturation episode post-nasogastric tube (NGT) placement, accompanied by pain and a right-sided pneumothorax.
- **Past Medical History**: None explicitly stated.
- **Personal History**: None provided.
- **Family History**: None provided.
- **Physical Examination**: Clinical signs not explicitly detailed.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Chest X-ray showed a moderate right pneumothorax and atypical placement of the NGT towards the right hypochondriac region.
  - Contrast-enhanced CT revealed NGT perforation of the left oropharynx, tracking through unusual anatomical spaces (left parapharyngeal, left visceral, retrotracheal, and retrodiaphragmatic regions).
  - Persistent right pneumothorax and right pleural effusion were noted on follow-up CT, suggestive of empyema.
  - Perioperative cultures confirmed *Curtibacterium acnes*.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Evaluation**:
   - The patient presented with a confirmed pneumothorax and atypical NGT course following placement. Initial concerns included NGT misplacement, potentially leading to airway or gastrointestinal tract injury.

2. **Evaluation of Pneumothorax and Imaging Confirmation**:
   - A CT scan was essential to confirm NGT misplacement. The imaging findings strongly suggested oropharyngeal perforation and NGT entry into deep neck and thoracic spaces, consistent with the abnormal distal positioning of the tube.
   - Esophageal or airway injury was considered but excluded in this case due to imaging findings pointing to pharyngeal perforation instead of esophageal/tracheal defects.

3. **Pneumothorax Complications**:
   - Persistent pneumothorax and pleural effusion raised concerns of empyema, confirmed via cultures showing *Curtibacterium acnes*. This required thoracic surgical intervention with decortication.

4. **Exclusion of Alternative Diagnoses**:
   - No evidence of tracheobronchial or gastrointestinal perforation based on imaging findings and clinical progression.
   - Endoscopic evaluation did not visualize perforation but was limited due to patient factors (e.g., secretions, positioning).

5. **Final Consideration**:
   - The recurrent pneumothorax and empyema were directly linked to oropharyngeal perforation caused by NGT misplacement, supported by imaging evidence.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Misplacement of the nasogastric tube resulting in oropharyngeal perforation, with the tube traversing parapharyngeal, visceral, retrotracheal, and retrodiaphragmatic spaces, ultimately causing recurrent pneumothorax and empyema complicated by *Curtibacterium acnes* infection.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Misplacement of nasogastric tube with oropharyngeal perforation, recurrent pneumothorax, and empyema.

results.content:
### Resoning:
In this case, we have a 51-year-old male who has experienced complications following abdominal hernia repair surgery. The primary issues include eventration, mesenteric laceration, and small bowel necrosis, which required intestinal resection and further procedures. Post-operatively, he was extubated on day 2 but then had a severe desaturation episode after nasogastric tube (NGT) placement, accompanied by pain and a right-sided pneumothorax.

Firstly, I need to understand the sequence of events and how they relate to each other. The patient underwent abdominal hernia repair, which is generally a routine procedure, but in this case, there were significant complications—eventration, mesenteric laceration, and small bowel necrosis. These are serious complications that suggest there might have been surgical errors or pre-existing conditions that complicated the surgery.

Eventration refers to the abnormal protrusion of abdominal contents through a weakened area in the abdominal wall. Mesenteric laceration can lead to bleeding and ischemia of the intestines, which might explain the small bowel necrosis. Intestinal resection was performed to manage this, which is standard care for necrotic bowel.

Post-operatively, the patient was extubated on day 2, which seems reasonable assuming his respiratory status was stable. However, after NGT placement, he experienced severe desaturation, pain, and a right-sided pneumothorax. This suggests that the NGT placement may have caused some injury leading to the pneumothorax.

A chest X-ray showed a moderate right pneumothorax and an atypically placed NGT in the right hypochondriac region. This indicates that the NGT may not have been placed correctly, possibly traversing unintended anatomical paths.

Contrast-enhanced CT revealed that the NGT had perforated the left oropharynx and tracked through unusual anatomical spaces, including the left parapharyngeal, left visceral, retrotracheal, and retrodiaphragmatic regions. This is a significant finding, indicating an aberrant route of the NGT.

Furthermore, follow-up CT showed a persistent right pneumothorax and right pleural effusion, suggestive of empyema. Empyema is an accumulation of pus in the pleural space, often due to infection.

Perioperative cultures confirmed the presence of *Curtibacterium acnes*, which is a commensal bacterium often found on skin and in mucous membranes. Its presence suggests a possible source of infection, potentially from the surgical site or from contamination during procedures.

Considering all these factors, I need to determine the primary diagnosis that encompasses the patient's current condition. The key elements are:

1. Postoperative complications from abdominal hernia repair: eventration, mesenteric laceration, small bowel necrosis, and subsequent intestinal resection.

2. Post-procedural complications: incorrect NGT placement leading to pneumothorax and possible pleural infection.

3. Infection with *Curtibacterium acnes*.

Given this information, the primary diagnosis would be:

Complications following abdominal hernia repair surgery, including eventration, mesenteric laceration, small bowel necrosis, and intestinal resection, complicated by misplacement of nasogastric tube leading to pneumothorax and empyema, with infection due to *Curtibacterium acnes*.

However, to be more precise, I need to prioritize the most immediate and life-threatening condition. In this case, the pneumothorax and empyema are urgent issues that require immediate attention due to their impact on respiratory function and the risk of sepsis.

Therefore, the primary diagnosis should focus on the respiratory complications arising from the misplacement of the NGT, leading to pneumothorax and empyema.

But, considering that these respiratory complications occurred in the context of major abdominal surgery and its complications, it's essential to address both the abdominal and respiratory issues in the diagnosis.

Alternatively, considering that *Curtibacterium acnes* was cultured from perioperative samples, it suggests that the infection may be related to the surgical procedure, possibly from contamination of the surgical site or from endogenous flora.

Moreover, the unusual placement of the NGT, perforating the oropharynx and traversing multiple anatomical spaces, indicates a procedural error during its insertion, which then led to the pneumothorax and subsequent empyema.

Given these points, I need to consider if there is a primary underlying issue that precipitated all these events.

First, the abdominal hernia repair led to several complications, including eventration, mesenteric laceration, and small bowel necrosis, which required intestinal resection. This suggests that the surgery was complicated by technical errors or unforeseen anatomical variations.

Second, the postoperative placement of the NGT was mishandled, leading to its misplacement and subsequent perforation of the oropharynx and penetration into deeper tissues, eventually causing pneumothorax and empyema.

Therefore, the misplacement of the NGT seems to be an independent error that compounded the patient's condition post-operatively.

However, the infection with *Curtibacterium acnes* could be related to either the abdominal surgery or the NGT placement, as this bacterium can be introduced via surgical instruments or from the patient's own skin flora.

Given that the NGT traversed multiple anatomical spaces and potentially introduced oral flora into the pleural space, it's possible that *Curtibacterium acnes* was introduced during this procedure.

Alternatively, it could have been introduced during the abdominal surgery.

To clarify, *Curtibacterium acnes* is known to cause infections in surgical sites, particularly in orthopedic surgeries, but it can also be associated with abdominal surgeries and other types of infections.

In this patient's case, the presence of *Curtibacterium acnes* in perioperative cultures suggests that it may be the causative agent of the empyema.

Therefore, the diagnostic focus should be on managing the empyema caused by *Curtibacterium acnes*, while also addressing the complications from the abdominal surgery and the misplacement of the NGT.

Moreover, given the atypical placement of the NGT, it's important to consider if there are any other complications related to the perforation of the oropharynx and the traversal through various anatomical spaces.

For instance, there could be injury to vessels or other organs along the path of the NGT, which might not have been immediately apparent but could present with delayed symptoms.

However, based on the information provided, the immediate concerns are the pneumothorax and empyema.

In summary, the patient has multiple complications:

1. Postoperative complications from abdominal hernia repair: eventration, mesenteric laceration, small bowel necrosis, and intestinal resection.

2. Misplacement of nasogastric tube leading to oropharyngeal perforation, pneumothorax, and empyema.

3. Infection with *Curtibacterium acnes*.

The primary diagnosis should address the most life-threatening condition, which in this case is the empyema secondary to misplacement of the nasogastric tube, complicated by *Curtibacterium acnes* infection.

However, considering the extensive abdominal complications, the patient requires a multidisciplinary approach involving both abdominal and respiratory specialists.

### Answer:
Complications following abdominal hernia repair surgery, including eventration, mesenteric laceration, small bowel necrosis, and intestinal resection, complicated by misplacement of nasogastric tube leading to pneumothorax and empyema due to *Curtibacterium acnes* infection.

<Step 2> A CT scan was performed to confirm NGT misplacement, revealing oropharyngeal perforation and the tube's entry into deep neck and thoracic spaces, consistent with abnormal distal positioning.
<Step 3> Imaging findings excluded esophageal or airway injury, instead pointing to pharyngeal perforation as the cause of the abnormal NGT positioning.
<Step 4> Persistent pneumothorax and pleural effusion raised concerns of empyema, which was confirmed via cultures identifying *Curtibacterium acnes*.
<Step 5> Thoracic surgical intervention, including decortication, was required to address the empyema caused by the pneumothorax complications.
<Step 6> Alternative diagnoses, such as tracheobronchial or gastrointestinal perforation, were excluded based on imaging findings and clinical progression.
<Step 7> Endoscopic evaluation did not visualize a perforation, but its utility was limited due to patient factors such as secretions and positioning.
<Step 8> The recurrent pneumothorax and empyema were directly linked to oropharyngeal perforation caused by NGT misplacement, as supported by imaging evidence.

<Step 9> Final diagnosis: Misplacement of the nasogastric tube resulted in oropharyngeal perforation, with the tube traversing parapharyngeal, visceral, retrotracheal, and retrodiaphragmatic spaces, ultimately causing recurrent pneumothorax and empyema complicated by *Curtibacterium acnes* infection.
